text: Sodium-ion batteries have become a new focus of research as an important electrochemical energy storage technology. However, it is hard to prepare the cathode materials with both high energy and low strain. Here, we report a new cathode material P2-layered lithium dual-site-substituted Na0.7Li0.03[Mg0.15Li0.07Mn0.75]O2 (NMLMO), in which Li ions are at both transition-metal (TM) and alkali-metal (AM) sites. Ex-situ XAS, in-situ XRD and EIS measurements were employed to verify the benefits of this lithium substitution. The results indicate that LiTM can enhance the capacity derived from oxygen anionic redox due to the Na-O-Li electronic configurations, while LiAM acts as the LiO6 prismatic pillars to maintain the stability of layered structure by preventing the structure from phase transformation. Correspondingly, NMLMO cathode material shows a specific capacity of 266 mAh g-1 and owns a nearly zero-strain characteristic within the voltage range of 1.5-4.6 V. Thus, the lithium dual-site-substituted strategy can also break the limitation of the capacity-stability trade-off in cathode materials for advanced rechargeable batteries.

The results indicate that LiTM can enhance the capacity derived from oxygen anionic redox due to the Na-O-Li electronic configurations, while LiAM acts as the LiO6 prismatic pillars to maintain the stability of layered structure by preventing the structure from phase transformation. Correspondingly, NMLMO cathode material shows a specific capacity of 266 mAh g-1 and owns a nearly zero-strain characteristic within the voltage range of 1.5-4.6 V.
